speaker: interviewer
text: The name of the survivor is Dr. Gideon Frieder. The interviewer is Gustav Goldberger, in the city of Bethesda, Maryland. And the language is English. Today's date is November 17, 1996. The survivor's name is Dr. Gideon Frieder. And the interviewer is Gustav Goldberger, in the city of Bethesda, Maryland, United States. The language is English. Good morning. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Gideon Frieder, or Gidon Frieder, the way I pronounce it.

speaker: interviewer
text: On behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I want to thank you so much for letting us interview you at this time. Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Zvolen, in Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the 30th of September, 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time of your birth, was there a family, your parents, who were living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I was the first child. My mother and father lived in Zvolen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your father's-- what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Armin [? Avraham ?] [? Abba ?] Frieder. He was the rabbi in Zvolen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother's name was Ruzena or [? Rogi ?] Frieder. Her maiden name was Berl, B-E-R-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were the first born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the firstborn, followed three years later by my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Gita Frieder.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- when you were born around that time frame, was there an extended family as well, grandparents, uncles, aunts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The maternal-- both the maternal and the fraternal grandparents were alive. There was extended family on both sides. My father had-- had a-- a brother, which is still alive. And my mother had sisters and brothers. Most of them died just-- just very recently. One of them is still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the-- your father's brother that's still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's brother who's still alive, his name is Emmanuel. And he lives in Israel, in Netanya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he live in-- in this same city at that time, where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not to my knowledge. The fraternal grandparents came from Prievidza. And I assume that he lived there or-- or around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your earliest recollections about life, as you remember, personally, during that period of time? How-- how old were you when you first became conscious of your existence?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say around one-- two, three years old, or something of this nature. I do not recall anything about Zvolen, except the fact that my-- that I was born there. My first recollections are from our residence, in Nove Mesto. My father became a rabbi in Nove Mesto. Miraculously, the certificate of his appointment is still available. And I have it with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have it here today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it here today in my house. It's framed.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the work that your father was engaged in when you were born, you say he was a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a rabbi in Nove Mesto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How early, or when did he become a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the date, although that is recorded on the document, but before my recollection, of anything about myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know now approximately what year you moved to Nove Mesto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But we can ascertain it from the document. I don't recall it as we speak. But it had to be in the '40s, beginning of 40's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you were born in '37?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born at the end of '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were about three or four years old when you came to Nove Mesto?

speaker: interviewee
text: About that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, if anything, do you remember about Nove Mesto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember that we lived in a large house. Although for a child, everything seems large.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Recently, my visit, I just couldn't make myself go back to Nove Mesto. So I didn't see the house. But people reported it was called the Hertzegova Villa, or the Villa of the Hertzog, of the Duke, the local nobleman. It has a large garden. There were animals in the garden. I remember playing with a-- a little sheep in the garden. There was a fence around the garden, which I remember. I remember vividly, there were no paved roads. And I remember that opposite that house, there was a smaller house, either grocery or some kind of food place. Because I was fond going down into the cellar, which had permanent ice. There were no refrigerators at that time. But cellars, in Slovakia, if they were deep enough, had permanent ice. Yeah. And there were food-- food stuff there. I remember going to the synagogue with my father, and sitting on his lap on the mizrah, so-called, on the-- facing the-- the congregation. That's how in Orthodox, in Neolog synagogues, really, things were going on. I recall later on going to school, first grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a school was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish school. It was a regular first grade Jewish school for Jews only. I recall very well that I had to walk. And at certain roads, I wouldn't walk through. Because the kids would beat me, because I was Jewish. So I wouldn't go through certain neighborhoods to go home. The-- the straight, quick road, was dangerous for me. So I didn't go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you be walking with other children, or would you walk by yourself? Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd walk by myself. I don't recall that any other children lived in the direction that I walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have an understanding about anti-Semitism in those early years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't call it understanding. I would call it awareness of the fact that Christians would beat Jews. I didn't attribute it to any anti-Semitism in that sense. I just know that Christians beat Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you deal with it? Did you go to your parents and complain about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall that I complained. I think that for them, it was clear that Christians would beat Jews. They just seen to it that I don't go through that neighborhood. I sometimes was stubborn enough to go. And sometimes I went through. And sometimes I was beaten. It-- I did not conceive it to be life-threatening, in that sense. I don't think I had a conception of threat of life throughout that period.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it more in the form of teasing, things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was beating. It was shoving. It was not beating with-- with clubs. Or-- it was children, more or less my age, a bit older. It was shoving and beating, and bloodying your knees, and your nose, and falling down, and scraping, and-- on that level.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember any friends. I remember that-- that we went to families that there were children there. But I cannot recall anybody by name. I met somebody who-- whose parents were apparently friends of my parent. And the parent, I stayed in his house, is today, the curator of the Jewish museum in Bratislava. And he told me about the recollections that his father told him, not-- not his own. [INAUDIBLE]. But I don't recall any friends from that time. I couldn't tell you a name, for example. I couldn't even tell you how big the class was. I tried to recall it, but I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish school. It was not a public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the subjects you were learning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- I know for a fact that I was learning how to read and write, because I was a voracious reader.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language?

speaker: interviewee
text: Slovak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any Hungarian taught as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was only Slovak and-- and Hebrew. And I was able to read Hebrew and Slovak. Although I wouldn't-- I wasn't able to understand the Hebrew. It was just reading, for prayer and writing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your father and mother, did they speak Slovak or Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: They spoke fluently Slovak, Hungarian, and German.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in the house, if you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house, they spoke all three languages. And I picked up some German. None of it-- of the-- the German I picked up even more after the war. But I was cognizant of the three languages as I was very young. And people just switch between the languages. Outside we always spoke Slovak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that for a reason having to do with-- with the people living in the surrounding areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I really cannot tell you. I think it was very natural to speak Slovak when everybody spoke Slovak. And between them, I interpreted that they switched languages so I will no understand. I did not attribute to it any value at the time, at least. I didn't even know about the Austro-Hungarian Empire and, you know, the heritage of languages that people had. But being multilingual, was for me, a very natural thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The synagogue that you were talking about, your father was a rabbi. Was it a large synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I recall it as a large synagogue. But again, the recollection of a small child, about size, is very misleading. I also recall my-- my father being a huge man. But I don't think he was. Because seeing my-- my uncle, I think that my brother-- my uncle had approximately the same body structure. He's not a tall man. But as a child, I considered him a giant.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how large a Jewish population was it, if you now know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not follow up that question. The-- the population was large. Because there was a place called Ohel David, Ohel David, which was an old age home, which was used to shelter Jews from various prosecution. And that increased-- inflated a bit, the size of the population, I think. It was large enough for me to think it's a large congregation. There were lots of people there on Shabbat evening and-- and holidays. And for me, it was the whole city. I know that-- that children ran around. And it was a large, large gathering, for a small child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you-- you mentioned, Neolog. And as I understand it, there are-- there were three types of Judaism. You had the Orthodox, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ultra-orthodox, then you had the status quo. And then you had the Neolog.

speaker: interviewee
text: Status quo onto the Neolog yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father was in charge of what kind of a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the status quo ante, but he-- he was on the Orthodox side of the status quo ante. He was Orthodox side of the Neolog. And in the book that was written about him, his struggle to orthodoxize, so to speak, to make more conservative the Neolog movement, are very evident. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was the 'Conservadox' type of thing in American terms, if you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were there other synagogues in Nove Mesto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure there were, but I was not aware of them. At the time I was not aware of any dichotomy, or any tension in Judaism. Later on, it became apparent to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother did-- your sister, rather, she-- she was born in Nove Mesto, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she was born in Nove Mesto. She was three years younger than I am. We were ascertained from the dates of the appointment, when it was done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she also-- do you remember whether she went to school or was she too young?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was too young at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was too young. She died very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other family in Nove Mesto or were you the only ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: The maternal grandparents eventually was-- were brought to Nove Mesto to [? Eldavit. ?] So I met them frequently.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Max Berl. Her name was Hermina Berl. He died during the Holocaust. She survived the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long did she survive beyond the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall exactly. I would estimate about 10 years, because she did come to Israel. And I had the opportunity to spend time with her, in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first become aware that there was a-- a problem with Germans coming in to Slovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father was absent a long time. And eventually, we had to flee. Also-- also at the end of '43, '44, I visited with my father many times in various concentration camps. These were not concentration camps in the sense of Germany. These were-- they were called working camps, more the detention and work, than-- than concentration. And I-- I recall that I was still with my father and couldn't understand very well why these people are there and not in homes. But nobody bothered to explain it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did your father explain why it was that he-- he went to these labor camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We just came to visit, because he was a rabbi. And he visited many places. I didn't know that he was part of the working group. That word was not even mentioned. I became aware of it only after that war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned the working group. What did it consist of? Or who were the members of the working group?

speaker: interviewee
text: That working group, or [SLOVAK], as they call them in Slovak, was kind of an underground government of the Slovak Jewry. It consisted of representative of various branches of Judaism, including the Orthodox, the Zionist movement. The names I recall, [? Giza ?] [? La ?] [? Fleischman, ?] [? Dovais ?] [? Mendel, ?] [INAUDIBLE], my father, and two others. They are in the book. I just don't recall the name off-hand. I-- until very recently, as a matter of fact, until the time that my uncle wrote the book about my father, I didn't go back to these memories. They-- they were not something I touched. I didn't talk about them. I didn't-- my-- my wife, for many years, we married in 1960, so married many years now. Although she knew I'm a survivor, she never read my story, so to speak. She never inquired. So I didn't dwell on-- on names and recollections. I didn't read the book I gave you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did not read it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just for the record, what is the name of the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the book in Hebrew is, [HEBREW]. It was translated to English under the name,

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was written by Emanuel.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was written by Emanuel Frieder, my uncle, based on the diaries of my father, and based on his recollections, and-- and his work after my father died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it published?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, I-- I can go back and look. We-- I have the original book here. I think the '80s.

speaker: interviewer
text: The--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was translated also to Slovak, by the way. Sorry to interrupt you.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right. You-- you indicated that it was a routine for your father to visit these labor camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would he stay there for a period of time, or just in and out? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I recall at least once that we stayed overnight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I don't know when I was not with him, how long he will stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any recollections at all what the camps looked like and who was there? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I cannot tell you. I-- I-- we came. We usually went to a social occasion. I remember clearly once, we went to possibly one of the festivals. Because there was a celebration and there was a-- even a-- a-- a performance. And I even remember a phrase out of the performance. But I was too young, and too many things happened since then, which overshadow that experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned, was there more than one camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of those camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sered, Vyhne, and one more, but I don't recollect. These were all in Slovakia. At the time already Slovakia, was separate from-- it was a separate country.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was forced labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was forced labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know who placed them in those camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not of my own recollection. I read-- I mean, I read a lot about the history since then. And it's all secondhand. I mean, it's all in the history books.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it the Germans or the Slovaks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the Slovaks. The Slovak government cooperated clearly with the German government. And there's voluminous documentation in my father's memoirs which are now in-- in Yad Vashem, voluminous documents showing the-- the clear cooperation. I would even say the willingness and eagerness of the Slovaks.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people that were placed in those camps, were they, in part, from Nove Mesto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could not tell you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have some idea of the magnitude of-- and number of people in those camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But the-- the numbers are documented very well in those memoirs. As I said, it's part of the archives of Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other experiences do you recall that had to do with the Germans, or the Slovaks, on behalf of the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Nove Mesto. As a matter of fact, there were no-- no Germans in Nove Mesto, to the best of my recollection. I don't recall any German from Nove Meso And indeed, the-- and the German army really invaded, in force, into Slovakia, only at the opposite-- onset, not opposite-- the onset of the Slovak National Uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44 That was in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: But until then, you say Nove Mesto was pretty clear of trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say it was clear of trouble. It was clear of Germans. There were the Hlinka Guard, which was the Slovak equivalent of the SA, I would say, or the SS. And they were clearly visible. And it was clear that I should avoid them. And everybody should avoid them, as much as one can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were Slovak gatherings that we had to avoid.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the Jews treated during that period? And I'm saying before the Germans actually invaded, by the Slovak gentiles.

speaker: interviewee
text: From my recollection, or from my knowledge?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from my recollection as a child, the only thing I can tell you is being beaten by-- by the gentiles, so to speak, having no recourse to help, of any adult on the street. If there was an adult, he or she would not interfere, clearly not on my behalf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: From my knowledge, the rules, the-- the legislature in Slovakia, was following pretty much a Nuremberg legislature. They were willing participants. I have quotes from Tiso.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tiso was the leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tiso was the leader of Slovakia [? then. ?] I would not call him the Fuhrer of Slovakia, but he was the leader of Slovakia. He was a Catholic priest. And there are many quotes from him, supporting the legislature. He was clearly a willing participant. His government was full of willing participants. It was clear economic value, to the Slovaks, in depriving the Jews. And in some quotes of Tiso, he specifically said there's nothing bad that the Jews will give back to Slovak what they took get away from them. Although, you know, doing well economically doesn't mean that you take back from anybody. But that was the attitude. I had a tremendous amount of bitterness against the Slovaks.

speaker: interviewer
text: For that reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: For their willingness to participate as quislings, as people who follow. It was not-- I don't justify the Germans by anyway. But it's a very different story. The Germans are guilty in a different way. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Slovaks did it from pure hatred. And I think they continue doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any present knowledge as to why it was that they had this passionate hatred towards the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Slovakia is a very strongly Catholic country. The Catholic Church was never pro-Jewish. So although the history in-- in Europe, even till this day, their passion plays in Germany, just clear anti-Semitic. And in other places, Europe was always anti-Semitic. I would even say they got it with their mother's milk. I am [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very bitter about the fact that the Catholic Church promoted and supported such approach, and other parts of Christianity did not stand up against it. And there are places which did stand up. And we-- and we all know about the Danes, and the Dutch. And-- that was not the story of all Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I had a Dane co-worker, which-- which said that the Danes-- how did he say it? The Danes are ashamed that they didn't do enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: What an irony.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a daily basis, again during that period of time before the Germans came in, how were the Jews affected by it, as it relates to the economy and making a living, and just the day-to-day involvements?

speaker: interviewee
text: It followed-- it followed the German-- it followed the German model. Jewish businesses were identified, were robbed, and not protected. Gentiles were asked not to buy from Jews, who are the suckers of the blood of the Slovak nation, the regular type of German copying. The Hlinka Guard was very eager to-- to be [? as ?] perceived as strong as SA or the SS. It just followed the same patterns, nothing innovative, nothing new. Concentration camp, deportations, the Slovaks were paid for everybody who was deported. And they were willing participants in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have to wear a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Jews had to wear yellow stars. I had a yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember wearing a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a small yellow star, because I was a child. My parents had a bigger yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father did not have a business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. He was the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: As the rabbi of the community, what did he specifically do to-- to try to ease the plight of the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a whole set of books on-- on his deeds. And I don't think we can-- we can summarize it here, for two reasons. A, it's lengthy, and B, I avoided reading those things. Suffice it to say, that in the Jewish Museum, in Bratislava, now on the Slovak non-Communist government, there's a large display of his work with his name and-- and portrait and pictures. He was awarded a-- he was awarded various honors by the Slovak pre-Communist government and by Jewish organizations. There was a large forest, of well above 1,000 trees, planted in his name, in Israel, by the survivors. His name is in the Jewish encyclopedia, not a big article, but an article mentioning him. And as I said, it's very, very well-documented in-- in his memoirs. [SIGHS] One thing he did, he stayed. He had the opportunity to leave. He-- he had a certificate to go to then Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He could have been alive today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the end of the '30s. He-- he was part of the Zionist movement, very unusual for rabbis at the time, to be part of the Zionist movement. Although, the Neologs were better in that sense. But he was a strong part of the Zionist movement, and he-- he had the opportunity to leave and go to Israel, well, then Palestine, under British mandate, since the First World War, till the end of the First World War. It was very difficult to got those certificates, to go legally. But he had-- he decided to stay, because he was shepherding his flocks, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of the tape number one.
